text: topsy-turvy stuff we’re already having to deal with these days, right? “That’s him,” the woman behind me said. The man pointed at me, his hand high over his head as if I couldn’t see it there. We were in a French farce, without the sexy element. For different reasons, none of us noticed the humor yet. As the couple clung to each other, for the first time I understood the nature of their fear. They weren’t a husband-and-wife cat burglar duo afraid of getting caught. They were more afraid of me than of the police. I considered their behavior, and the sofa fabric. And the kitchen cabinets facing me beside the front door, which had a maple finish rather than the cherry in my unit. My face must have flushed red. Every detail struck me at once, a face slap of logic I’d been denying. I’m guessing I had the kind of face a crazy person makes before they lunge for the knife block on the countertop. Trained officer eyes judged the situation at once, directed their correctional attentions my way… It’s too bad we couldn’t have extended the farce with a tennis ball back-and-forth: “It’s my apartment,” *thunk* “No, it’s our apartment” *thwack* “No it’s mine.” I’d have preferred comedy instead of the humiliation that followed. “I thought…” My shoulders slumped. “I must have…” I turned to the couple who lived here, looked to the ceiling, gestured at the open-concept kitchen and living area. “Confused,” I said, as if that was all the explanation needed. Did I mention how nice the other folks were dressed? I think they used that bedroom as a home office, and they were both in business casual. He wore a dress shirt with rolled-up cuffs, some Dockers-type pants and mesh-knit shoes with leather accents; she wore a striped shirt with an open collar, peach-colored capri pants and matching flats. Maybe they were overdressed for online work sessions, where only head and shoulders showed in the frame, but their wardrobe definitely made a better impression on law enforcement. This wasn’t one of my meeting days, and I’d dressed for a sweaty outdoor stroll—yesterday’s unironed T-shirt with a Spaghetti-O stain over my stomach, and the pair of jeans I’d been wearing all week. I’d rinsed my over-long hair recently, but might not have used shampoo. My broken, confused speech probably hadn’t helped. I needed to raise my eloquence level. “Occam’s razor,” I said. God, Mira, don’t start laughing again. This is the part where you’re supposed to feel sorry for me, okay? I thought if I spoke like an egghead, they’d give me a little more respect, but I’ll admit I came off like a fool. I might even have said “principle of parsimony.” I was trying to explain why I’d been so clueless. I’d started from the wrong premise, ignored anything that contradicted it. Instead of going to the simple and obvious solution—I’d walked into the wrong apartment unit—I piled on preposterous explanations. Multiplying entities without necessity, as the definition goes. Strangers snuck into my apartment and changed furniture, repainted walls and refinished cabinets in a matter of minutes—unlikely even if I’d been drugged or had fallen asleep for a week, a month; even if I’d slipped into a parallel universe where I had different decorating tastes. I got all theoretical, said something about wormholes and altered perception. Over his mask, Officer Grey Streak raised a knowing eyebrow at his partner. That eyebrow said, “drug addict.” “No, really, I do live here.” I sounded like someone repeating a debunked delusion. But by “here,” I meant the building, rather than the specific apartment I now stood in. “I can prove it.” I patted my front pocket. Felt my phone there, but I was looking for my wallet. “Apartment 302,” I said. My wallet was gone. I’d worn my loose pants—the pair I’ve been wearing a lot these days. They’re comfortable around the house. Around my waist, too. The ill-fitting pants were usually fine, since I only met co-workers once a week, my head and shoulders boxed the size of a postage stamp on a crowded meeting screen. Comfortable pants for lounging, but not the best way to secure my belongings. “I seem to have lost…” I trailed off, my credibility shot to hell. They might as well cuff me and drag me to the station. “It’s over there,” the gal tenant said. “On the floor in front of the couch.” I turned around and sure enough, my wallet—right where it had fallen out. You know, when I was crawling around on the floor reaching under a stranger’s sofa. “Thank you.” I practically walked backwards to retrieve it, my hands up where the officers could see them. The woman had stepped away from her husband, or he had stopped bothering to protect her. They were more confident now that the police had arrived. Or maybe because they recognized me. Before the lockdown, we’d have run into each other in the building’s shared courtyard, or at a “community grievance” event where we complained about things the management would never fix—like lights in the hallway, the broken package shelves, the pet-stained rug in the lobby. I was pretty vocal at those gatherings. I’m sure that’s where the couple remembered me from. You’re right, Sis. Sometimes there’s an advantage to being a loudmouth. I found my Driver’s License and held it up. One of the officers lifted a rubber-gloved finger and traced lines in the air above the card. I watched his eyes flit back and forth while reading, then over the top of the card and down while comparing my disheveled, masked face to the five-year-old DMV photo. “302,” I said. When Gray Streak was finished examining it, I offered the card to his partner, who declined. I turned to let the tenants inspect the printed address, but they waved it away. Basically, they seemed ready for me to leave. The lead officer’s tone shifted to wrap-it-up. He summed
